flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf): the 2x4 parts need SEVEN 8 ft 2x4s, not the six listed. Each 61" tabletop board leaves only 35" of its board, and neither the 64" trim nor the 48-1/2" end trim fits in a 35" offcut or alongside each other (64 + 48-1/2 = 112-1/2" > 96"). Lower bound is 7 boards and it is provable.
LUMBER SHORTFALL: the two 49-1/2" shelf sides total 99" and cannot both come from one 96" 1x6; the plan lists one Pine, 1x6, 8 ft. packBoards returns 2 boards (provable).
TIGHT TO IMPOSSIBLE: 'Bottom of shelf' is 48" long and the only 1x12 listed is a 4 ft (48") board — zero margin for squaring either end. A 5 ft board would be safe.

NO SAW IN THE TOOL LIST. The plan has 17 cut-list rows including 45deg and 30deg end miters, but tools[] contains no miter saw, circular saw, or table saw. The inserted cut step therefore tags only layout tools.

Shelf-end length is inconsistent: the shelf sides at 49-1/2" = the 48" bottom plus 3/4" of stock at each end, so the sides clearly run the full outside length — but the shelf ENDS are 11-1/2" while the bottom is 11-1/4" wide, a 1/4" discrepancy the data does not resolve. The rewrite says to cut the ends to fit the assembled box.

Leg-assembly spacing is not determined: the 13" 2x2 top support and the 15-1/2" 1x4 bottom differ by 2-1/2" and the source never says whether either laps the posts or fits between them. The rewrite describes both without asserting a finished leg width.

Tabletop trim layout is under-specified: only the 64" front piece is derivable (61" top plus 1-1/2" of trim at each end). Where the 48-1/2", 19", 13", 4" and 14" trim pieces land is not stated anywhere in the source; the rewrite says to fit them and does not invent positions.
The original tabletop step says '2" pocket hole screws' but tags the '2-1/2" pocket hole screws' material; the plan lists no 2" pocket screw. Corrected to 2-1/2" in the rewrite.
Foot orientation relative to the leg assembly (which way the 19-1/2"/21-1/2" pair runs) is not stated in the source.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: Cut everything before you assemble anything, working from the cut list. From the 2x4s: five tabletop boards at 61", one 64" trim, one 48-1/2" end trim, one 19" end short trim, one 13" short trim, two foot top pieces at 19-1/2" with both ends at 45deg, and two foot bottom pieces at 21-1/2" with both ends 30deg off square — on both foot pairs the stated length is long point to long point, so set the saw once and cut both of a pair without moving the fence. From the 1x4s cut four leg fronts and backs at 30-3/4", two bottom-of-leg pieces at 15-1/2", and four 3-1/2" square soles; from the 1x3s cut four leg sides at 30-3/4"; from the 2x2 cut two top-of-leg supports at 13". From the 1x12 cut the 48" shelf bottom, from the 1x6 two shelf sides at 49-1/2" and two shelf ends at 11-1/2", and from the 2x6 a 14" front piece for the towel bar plus a 4" short piece. Check your stock before the first cut: each 61" tabletop board consumes a whole 8 ft 2x4, and neither the 64" nor the 48-1/2" trim will fit in one of those offcuts or share a board with the other, so the 2x4 parts need seven 8 ft boards rather than the six listed — and the two 49-1/2" shelf sides total 99", so one 8 ft 1x6 will not cover them either.

ops.body: Pair one 19-1/2" top piece with one 21-1/2" bottom piece and glue and screw them face to face with 2-1/2" screws, so the shorter piece sits on top of the longer one and the splayed profile falls back from the ground on both ends. Build two of these — one per leg assembly. Keep the long points of the two pieces aligned to the same reference end as you clamp, because the angled ends are what set the splay and a 1/4" offset there shows plainly on a finished piece. Then sand the exposed end grain and the sharp corners into a soft round, starting coarse and stepping down through the grits; the worn, radiused end is the vintage detail and a crisp factory edge reads wrong.

Glue and fasten a 3-1/2" square 1x4 sole flat under each end of both foot assemblies — four soles total, two per foot. The soles are what the console actually stands on, so set them flush with the outside of the foot's long point and check that both ends of a foot sit in the same plane before the glue grabs. Use 1-1/4" brad nails, or short screws, driven up through the sole into the foot so nothing shows on the floor side.

Each leg is a hollow post 30-3/4" tall, built from two 1x4 faces at 30-3/4" and two 1x3 sides at 30-3/4"; running the 2-1/2" 1x3 sides between the 3-1/2" 1x4 front and back gives a post 3-1/2" wide by 4" deep. Build four identical posts. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the long edges of the 1x3 sides and drive 1-1/4" pocket screws from inside the post outward into the 1x4s, so every screw hole is trapped inside the hollow and none of it shows once the post is closed up. Clamp each post at both ends and at the middle while you drive, or the long thin faces will bow away from the sides and the post will finish out of square.

Join the four posts into two leg assemblies of two posts each. The 13" 2x2 support ties the pair together at the top and the 15-1/2" 1x4 ties them at the bottom; note that these two lengths differ by 2-1/2", so decide before you drill whether each piece fits between the posts or laps across their outside faces, and use the same choice on both assemblies. Fasten with 2" screws and glue, driving through the posts into the 2x2 and through the 1x4 into the posts. Check each assembly for square across its diagonals before the glue sets — the top support is what the tabletop later screws into, so a twisted assembly puts the top out of level.

Stand one foot assembly under each leg assembly and fasten it with 2-1/2" screws driven down through the bottom of each post into the foot. Center the foot side to side under the pair of posts so the splay reaches out evenly in both directions. Set both finished leg assemblies on a flat floor together and confirm they stand at the same height and neither rocks before you go on; correcting a foot later means taking the whole base apart.

The shelf is a shallow open tray. The two 49-1/2" 1x6 sides run the full outside length and the 48" 1x12 bottom sits between them — 48" plus 3/4" of side stock at each end is exactly the 49-1/2" side length, which is what fixes the assembly this way round. Drill pocket holes along both long edges of the bottom, stand a side against each edge with the bottom's face flush to the sides' bottom edges, and drive 1-1/4" pocket screws. Clamp the pair as you go so the tray does not spring out of parallel down its length.

Close the two open ends of the tray with the 11-1/2" 1x6 shelf ends, pocket-screwed into both the bottom and the sides with 1-1/4" screws. Measure the actual opening at each end of the assembled tray before you fasten these — the cut list gives the ends at 11-1/2" while the bottom is 11-1/4" wide, so trim to the real opening rather than forcing the stated length in. Set the ends flush with the bottom's underside so the shelf sits flat on the leg supports later.

Lay the five 61" 2x4s on edge, side by side, to make a top 61" long and 17-1/2" wide (five boards at 3-1/2"). Drill pocket holes along the mating edges of four of the boards, spacing them every 8" to 10" and staggering them so screws from adjacent seams do not collide, then draw the boards together with 2-1/2" pocket screws and glue. Work on a flat surface and clamp each seam flush across the face before driving, because any step between boards has to be sanded out of a 1-1/2" thick top afterward.

Trim the tabletop with the 2x4 pieces, gluing and screwing each with 2-1/2" screws driven through the trim into the edge of the top. The 64" piece is the one length the geometry fixes: 61" of tabletop plus 1-1/2" of trim at each end, so it caps the full front and the end trim tucks in behind it. Fit the 48-1/2", 19" and 13" pieces to the remaining edges by measuring the assembled top rather than trusting the stated lengths — the source does not say which edge each one covers. Keep every trim piece flush with the top face so the whole surface sands as one plane.

Fasten the 14" 2x6 to the front of the trimmed top with 2-1/2" screws and glue, and add the 4" 2x6 short piece alongside it. This is the board the towel bar mounts to, so drive the screws where the bar's mounting plates will not land on them. Mark the bar's mounting-hole centers off the bar itself, drill pilot holes, and mount it — pine strips easily, so pilot every hole rather than driving straight into the face.

Set the tabletop upside down on a padded surface, stand both leg assemblies on it in position, and drive 2-1/2" screws up through the 2x2 top-of-leg supports into the underside of the top. Slide the shelf tray into place between the leg assemblies and fasten it through the leg posts. Turn the console upright, sand the whole piece through the grits — a 2x4 top takes real sanding to level the seams — and apply your finish.

baseStepTitles: Attach the feet to the legs
Attach the final trim and towel bar
Join the base to the top and finish

flags: cutList is EMPTY and the plan is unpublished. There is not a single authored dimension in this file — the tray's length, width and side height are all left to the builder, and the description explicitly frames the length as table-dependent. The rewrite therefore adds technique and the relationships between the parts, and does NOT invent a size.
The only dimensions used are the standard actual size of the named 1x12 stock (3/4" x 11-1/4") and its stated 10 ft length. Everything else in the prose is a relationship, not a number.

The source says 'sides and bottom' but the handles go on 'the ends', so the tray evidently has four walls. Which pair runs the full outside length is NOT determined by any data in the file; the rewrite tells the builder to choose one and stay consistent, and describes the arithmetic either way.
No fasteners are listed — only wood glue. A glue-only tray is buildable but the rewrite notes that the joint has to be clamped properly because there is nothing mechanical backing it up.
A cut list with even one authored length would raise this plan a full point. Recommend authoring one.

ops.title: Size and cut the tray pieces
ops.body: Measure your table first and pick a tray length that leaves clear space at each end rather than running edge to edge — this is a centerpiece, not a runner. Cut the bottom to that length at the board's full 11-1/4" width, then rip the side and end stock for the walls from the rest of the same 10 ft board so the grain and color stay consistent across the whole tray, which is the entire point of building it from one board. Decide now which pair of walls runs the full outside length: if the long sides run through, the two ends are cut to the bottom's 11-1/4" width and fit between them; if the ends run through, each end is 11-1/4" plus 3/4" of side stock at each corner and the sides shorten to match. Cut all four walls to the same height so the top edge sits level all the way round.

If you want the softer edge, rout a chamfer along the top outside edge of each wall piece before assembly, while the parts are still flat and easy to clamp down — chamfering after glue-up means working around inside corners the bit cannot reach. Take it in two shallow passes rather than one deep one, and run a test cut on an offcut to set the depth. This is optional; a sanded, eased edge looks fine and needs no router.

Dry-fit all five pieces first and confirm the walls close square around the bottom before any glue goes on. Glue the walls to the bottom's edges and to each other at the corners, then clamp across the tray in both directions — with no nails or screws in this build the glue joint is the only thing holding it, so even clamping pressure and full glue coverage matter more than usual. Check the diagonals while it is still wet and nudge it square, then leave it clamped about an hour before handling.

Sand the whole tray smooth inside and out, including the bottom, and ease every edge and corner — this piece gets slid around a dining table, so a sharp bottom edge will mark the table and a sharp top edge will catch. Break the inside corners by hand where the sander cannot reach. Then stain or paint, finishing the underside as well as the top so the board takes on moisture evenly and does not cup.

Center a cabinet pull on each end wall, marking both mounting-hole centers off the pull itself rather than measuring twice. Drill the through-holes from the outside face and back the exit side with scrap so the plywood-thin 3/4" wall does not blow out on the inside. Check that the pull's screws are short enough not to protrude into the tray, and swap for shorter ones if they are.

flags: GEOMETRY (from scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs, treated as settled): the 84" Sides run the FULL length and the 46-1/2" Top and bottom sit BETWEEN them — proved by the 48" x 84" Back, which closes only on that reading. All prose follows this.

LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf, provable): the 15-3/4"-wide plywood parts need SEVEN 96" strips. A 4x8 sheet yields three 15-3/4" strips (47-1/4" of the 48" width), so two sheets give six. A third sheet is required, contradicting the material note 'roughly two sheets per organizer if the layout is planned well'. The two 84" sides and two 79" dividers alone consume four strips almost entirely.
HARDWARE SHORTFALL: 12 adjustable shelves need 4 shelf pins each = 48 pins; the materials list 24. Enough for 6 shelves.

DERIVED and stated in the prose: the toekick notch is 3-1/2" deep, because the 79" dividers equal 84" less the 3-1/2" notch less 3/4" of bottom and 3/4" of top. The source never states the notch depth directly.

DERIVED and stated in the prose: interior width is 46-1/2", and two 3/4" dividers leave 45" that splits exactly as 11" + 23" + 11" — matching the 11" small fixed shelves in the outer bays and the 23" large fixed shelves in the middle bay. The 10-3/4" adjustable shelves are the 11" bay less 1/4" of maneuvering clearance, so all twelve belong to the outer bays.

The source never states the shelf-pin hole spacing, the vertical extent of the pin-hole field, or the fixed shelves' heights. The rewrite gives the method and the reference-edge discipline but does not invent those numbers.

step.title: Rip the strips and cut the panels
step.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 15-3/4"-wide strips on the table saw — every carcass part except the toekick and the back comes off these strips, so a strip that wanders in width puts the whole tower out. From them cut two sides at 84", two top and bottom panels at 46-1/2", two dividers at 79", two large fixed shelves at 23", four small fixed shelves at 11", and twelve adjustable shelves at 10-3/4". Cut the 48" toekick from the 1x4 in your matching species and the 46-1/2" underneath support from the pine 1x4, and cut the 1/4" back to 48" x 84" — leave its factory corner intact, because that square corner is what you will register the carcass against later. Count your material before you start: those parts need seven 96" strips and a 4x8 sheet only yields three, so two sheets will leave you short and a third is needed.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species
1/4" plywood, matching species, for the back

ops.body: Notch the bottom front corner of both 84" side panels 3-1/2" high to clear the toekick — that depth is what makes the numbers close, since the 79" dividers are the 84" side less the 3-1/2" notch and less 3/4" of bottom panel and 3/4" of top panel. Then lay out the shelf-pin holes. Clamp both sides together face to face with their bottom ends flush and drill the two panels in one operation, or index every hole off the same bottom edge with a stop block, so a hole on the left side and its partner on the right are at identical heights. Use a depth stop on the bit — a pin hole drilled through a 3/4" panel shows on the outside of the tower and cannot be undone. This is the step where care beats speed: even a small offset side to side leaves every adjustable shelf sitting tilted, and there is no fixing it afterward.

Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of the 46-1/2" top and bottom panels and fasten them between the two sides with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, so the 84" sides run the full outside height and the tower finishes 48" wide — 46-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end, which is exactly the 48" width of the back panel. Set the bottom panel on top of the 3-1/2" toekick notch, not at the very bottom of the sides. Face the pocket holes where they will be hidden by the shelves and the back, and clamp each joint tight before driving, since plywood end grain will let a screw pull the panel out of flush.

Stand the two 79" dividers between the top and bottom panels and fasten them with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws top and bottom. Their positions are fixed by the shelves: the 46-1/2" interior less two 3/4" dividers leaves 45", which splits as an 11" bay, a 23" bay and an 11" bay — matching the 11" small fixed shelves in the outer bays and the 23" large fixed shelves in the middle. Measure each bay at both the top and the bottom of the divider and clamp a shelf offcut in as a spacer while you drive, or the dividers will bow and the adjustable shelves will bind partway up. Drill the shelf-pin holes in the divider faces now if you want adjustability in the outer bays — it is far easier before the back goes on.

Lay the tower face down, put the 48" x 84" back on, and align one factory edge and corner of the back to one side and to the top of the carcass. Nail through the back into every side, divider and fixed shelf with 3/4" finish nails, working outward from that registered corner and pulling the carcass into line with the back's edges as you go. The back is what squares the whole tower — a 1/4" panel cut square is a better reference than any measurement you can take across a 7 ft case.

Install the four 11" small fixed shelves in the outer bays and the two 23" large fixed shelves in the middle bay, fastening them through the sides and dividers. Then set the twelve 10-3/4" adjustable shelves on their pins: they are the 11" bay less 1/4", so they belong in the outer bays only and that quarter inch is the clearance that lets you angle one into place without scraping the finish off both walls. Buy more pins before this step — twelve adjustable shelves take four pins each, forty-eight in total, and the plan lists twenty-four. Set a shelf on its pins and check it for rock before loading the tower; a shelf that rocks means a pin hole is off and it is better found now.

baseStepTitles: Notch the sides and drill shelf-pin holes
Attach the top and bottom to the sides

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf, provable): the 2x4 parts — eight 19" tops, sixteen 18" sides and eight 3-1/2" blocks — total 468" and need FOUR 10 ft 2x4s. The plan lists three (360").

VERIFIED SUFFICIENT (packBoards, hand-checked layout): the five 8 ft 2x6s do cover the deck exactly — four boards each take two 26-1/4" outer boards plus two 21-3/8" middle boards (95-1/4" plus three kerfs), and the fifth takes all eight 11-7/8" inner boards (95" plus seven kerfs). The rewrite states this layout because it is the only one that fits.
VERIFIED SUFFICIENT: two 12 ft 4x4s cover the sixteen 16-1/2" legs (eight per board, 132" plus seven kerfs on 144"), and two 10 ft 1x4s cover the eight 26-1/4" trim pieces (four per board).

The 'Build the leg sets' step calls for 'screws and glue' but no glue appears in materials[]. The rewrite drops the glue reference rather than tagging a material that does not exist. Candidate to add an exterior glue line.

DERIVED and stated in the prose: seat height is 19-1/2" — a 16-1/2" 4x4 leg plus the 1-1/2" 2x4 top cap plus the 1-1/2" 2x6 deck. Also derived: a 19" top cap spanning two 3-1/2" legs leaves 12" between them.

DERIVED, held back from the prose as too indirect: the inner ring's 11-7/8" short-point boards produce roughly a 14" opening around the trunk. Confirm the tree will still fit that opening as it grows before building.

The source does not say how far the outer, middle and inner deck rings sit apart, nor how the sections fasten to each other at final assembly. The rewrite says to set the gaps at dry-fit and does not invent a spacing.

step.title: Cut every part first
step.body: This bench is eight identical sections, so cut in batches with a stop block rather than measuring each piece — identical parts are what make the octagon close. From the 4x4s cut sixteen legs at 16-1/2", eight per 12 ft board. From the 2x4s cut eight leg-set tops at 19", sixteen leg-set sides at 18" with one end beveled 22.5deg off square to the long point, and eight 3-1/2" blocks; note that these total 468" and will need a fourth 10 ft 2x4 beyond the three listed. From the 2x6s cut eight outer deck boards at 26-1/4" long point, eight middle at 21-3/8" long point, and eight inner at 11-7/8" short point, every end at 22.5deg off square — four of the boards each yield two outer and two middle pieces and the fifth yields all eight inner pieces, which is the only layout that fits five 8 ft boards. Cut the eight 26-1/4" deck trim pieces from the 1x4s, four per board, both ends beveled 22.5deg. For the hexagon variant, build six sections instead of eight and cut every 22.5deg angle at 30deg instead.

ops.body: Prestain every board now, before a single joint goes together, including the ends and both faces. Once the eight sections are joined into an octagon the inside faces are genuinely hard to reach, and a bench that is half-finished on the inside will weather unevenly there first. Stand the parts on edge or on stickers to dry rather than flat, so the down face does not stick and lift.

Stand two 16-1/2" 4x4 legs on end and lay a 19" 2x4 top across them, one flush to each end of the cap, which leaves 12" of open space between the two legs. Drive three 2-3/4" exterior screws through the cap into the end grain of each leg, spreading them across the 3-1/2" width rather than in a line so the joint cannot pivot. Build eight of these — six for the hexagon variant — and stack them together as you go to check that all eight came out the same height, since a leg set that is even 1/8" tall will show as a step in the finished deck. With the 1-1/2" cap and the 1-1/2" deck boards on top, each set puts the finished seat at 19-1/2".

Attach an 18" beveled side piece to each side of every leg set, mirrored so the two 22.5deg bevels lean toward each other — this pair of bevels is what lets eight sections meet edge to edge and close a full octagon, so getting the mirroring right on every set matters more than the fastening. Run the long point of each bevel to the outside face. Fasten with 2-3/4" exterior screws into the legs. Dry-hold two finished leg sets against each other before building the rest and confirm the bevels meet flush with no gap at the outside; if they do not, correct the saw angle now rather than after all eight are built.

Fit a 3-1/2" 2x4 block inside each leg set, between the top cap and the two side pieces, and screw through the cap and both sides into it with 2-3/4" exterior screws. This block is what turns a cap-and-two-sides joint into a solid corner — without it the sides can rack against the legs under someone sitting down and eventually work the screws loose. One block per set, eight in total.

Set all the leg sets out on flat, level ground in their octagon and lay the deck boards across them dry — the 26-1/4" outer ring, the 21-3/8" middle ring, and the 11-7/8" inner ring — before any deck fastener goes in. Check that each ring's mitered ends meet cleanly all the way round and that the accumulated error has not opened one joint into a wedge, and set the gap between the three rings now so it is consistent everywhere. This dry-fit is the whole reason for building on the ground first: laying up blind around the actual tree hides exactly the errors that are easiest to fix here and hardest to fix there. For the hexagon variant every deck end is cut at 30deg instead of 22.5deg.

Carry the sections to the tree and set them around the trunk in the order you dry-fitted them, then fasten the deck boards down to the leg-set caps with 2-3/4" exterior screws, two per board per bearing point. Work around the ring rather than finishing one section at a time, so any accumulated error spreads out instead of piling up in the last joint. Check the top for level as you go and shim under a leg set where the ground falls away. Leave clearance between the inner ring and the bark all the way round — the trunk will grow into whatever gap you leave.

Fasten the eight 26-1/4" 1x4 trim pieces around the outer edge of the deck, their 22.5deg bevels meeting at each corner, with 2-3/4" exterior screws. The trim covers the outer deck boards' end grain, which is where an outdoor bench takes on water first, so seat each piece tight to the deck edge and touch up the stain on any face you cut or scuffed during assembly.

flags: run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box for this plan, but the arithmetic is one-sided and is stated in the prose with its proof: the 28-1/2" tops and bottoms plus 3/4" of side stock at each end equal exactly the 30" width of the back panel, which only closes if the 33-1/2" sides run the full height and the 28-1/2" panels fit between them. Recorded here so it is visible as a derivation, not an authored value.

DIMENSION CONFLICT: two stacked 33-1/2" carcasses are 67" tall, but the back panel is specified at 68" long. The 1" is unexplained. The rewrite tells the builder to cut the back to the assembled stack rather than to the stated 68".

MATERIAL IS EXACTLY SUFFICIENT, NOT SHORT (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf): one 4x8 sheet rips into three 15-1/4" strips (45-3/4" of the 48" width) and the nine plywood parts pack into exactly those three 96" strips — two strips take 33-1/2" + 33-1/2" + 28-1/2" and the third takes three 28-1/2" pieces. Worst slack is 1/4", so a sloppy rip or an extra saw kerf will leave you short. Stated as a caution in the prose.
The five 28-1/2" bay supports pack comfortably into the two 1x3 8 ft boards (three per board).

The doors' placement is not determined: two 14-3/4" doors side by side span 29-1/2" of the 30" case, which is a full-overlay pair on ONE carcass, but the source never says which carcass, and their 30-3/4" height is 2-3/4" shorter than a carcass's 33-1/2". The rewrite gives the width arithmetic and tells the builder to fit to the actual carcass.
The source does not state how many bay supports go where beyond 'one per carcass plus a second in the base', which accounts for 3 of the 5 cut. The remaining two are unassigned in the data.
The bottom shelf's height within the base carcass is never given.

step.title: Rip the strips and cut the panels
step.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood sheet into 15-1/4"-wide strips first — every carcass part comes off these, so an inconsistent rip width shows up as a cabinet that will not close square. Cut the four carcass sides at 33-1/2" first, then the five 28-1/2" pieces that become the two carcass tops, the two bottoms and the fixed shelf. The material is exactly sufficient and no more: three strips take 33-1/2" plus 33-1/2" plus 28-1/2" on two of them and three 28-1/2" pieces on the third, which leaves about a quarter inch of slack in the worst strip, so measure twice and keep your kerfs tight. Cut the five bay supports at 28-1/2" from the 1x3s, three from one board and two from the other, and leave the 1/4" back panel oversized for now — you will trim it to the assembled cabinet rather than to a number.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet
1/4" underlayment or hardwood plywood, full sheet

ops.body: Build two identical boxes, a base carcass and a top carcass, each from two 33-1/2" sides and two 28-1/2" panels. The 28-1/2" panels fit between the sides, which run the full 33-1/2" height — 28-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end is exactly the 30" width the back panel is cut to, and that is what fixes the assembly this way round. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of the 28-1/2" panels and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws. Put the top carcass's pocket holes on its top edge and the base carcass's on its bottom edge, so that once the two boxes are stacked and joined every hole is either against the floor or above eye level and none of them shows on a visible face. Clamp each joint flush before driving; plywood end grain lets a screw pull a panel proud.

Fit a 28-1/2" 1x3 bay support across the back of each carcass, pocket holes drilled to face upward into the side panels, and fasten with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws — this is what keeps a 30"-wide plywood box from racking before the back goes on. The base carcass gets a second support, this one with its pocket holes facing the back of the cabinet. Plan and drill that second one before the shelf goes anywhere near it: it has to receive pocket holes from the underside of the fixed bottom shelf, and drilling into a support already buried inside an assembled carcass is much harder than doing it on the bench.

Fit the remaining 28-1/2" panel into the base carcass as the fixed bottom shelf, fastening it with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws driven up into its underside so no hole shows from above. Mark its height on both sides of the carcass from the same reference edge and clamp a straight offcut across as a ledger to hold it level while you drive. Check the shelf for level in both directions before the last screw goes in — a fixed shelf is not adjustable later.

Stack the two carcasses, join them, and then cut the 1/4" back panel to the assembled cabinet rather than to a stated size — two 33-1/2" carcasses stack to 67", not the 68" the cut list gives, so measure the real stack. Glue the back edges of both carcasses, lay a factory edge of the panel against one side of the cabinet and a factory corner at the top, and nail down with 3/4" brad nails working out from that corner, pulling each side and top into line with the panel's edge as you go. This is what squares the whole cabinet — a factory-cut 1/4" panel is a truer reference than anything you can measure across a 5-1/2 ft case, so do not clamp the carcass square first and hope the back agrees.

Fit the remaining shelves either fixed in place, pocket-screwed like the bottom shelf, or hung on pins for adjustability. If you go adjustable, trim about 1/8" off the shelf length first — a shelf cut to the exact opening cannot be angled into a closed carcass without scraping both side panels. Drill the pin holes with a depth stop and index every hole off the same bottom edge so the pairs on the two sides land at identical heights; a shelf that rocks means one hole is off.

Iron or apply the 3/4" edge banding to the door edges and trim it flush before hanging anything. The two 14-3/4" doors are meant to sit side by side over one carcass: together they span 29-1/2" of the cabinet's 30" width, leaving about 1/4" of reveal at each outer edge for a full overlay. Hold both doors up against the actual carcass before you commit to hinge positions — the doors are 30-3/4" tall against a 33-1/2" carcass, so where that extra 2-3/4" falls is a decision you make on the cabinet, not from the cut list. Mount the frameless full-overlay hinges, then use their built-in adjustment to bring the gap between the two doors even top to bottom.

flags: cutList is EMPTY and the plan is unpublished. The ONLY dimension anywhere in this file is the 3/8" dowel diameter. There is no pattern size, no body height, no base width, no wheel diameter — so the rewrite adds technique and sequence only, and deliberately contains no invented number.

The pattern itself is referenced but not supplied ('Print the camper-shaped pattern'). Without a printable pattern or at least an overall size, this plan is not buildable by reading alone no matter how the steps are written. That is the real blocker, not the prose.
Wood glue is used in two steps but is NOT in materials[] — only scrap wood, the dowel and paint are listed. The rewrite mentions glue as technique but tags no material for it. Candidate to add.

The source does not say how many body pieces there are. It says 'assemble the sides', implying two silhouettes with a base between them, and the rewrite follows that reading without asserting a count beyond what the source states.
The napkin capacity depends on the gap between the two sides, which nothing in the file specifies.

ops.body: Print the camper pattern at the size you want the finished holder and transfer it to your scrap stock — this is a silhouette project, so the pattern is the plan and there is no board-by-board measuring to do. If the holder has two matching sides, transfer the shape once and either trace the second from the first or stack the two blanks and cut them together, so the pair comes out identical. Scrap is genuinely the right material here since the whole piece gets painted, but pick something flat and free of knots along the curved cut lines.

Cut the curved camper body on the jigsaw, staying just outside the pattern line and sanding back to it rather than trying to hit the line with the blade — a jigsaw wanders on tight curves and a silhouette lives or dies on its outline. If you stacked two blanks, keep them clamped or taped together through the whole cut so both sides match exactly. Then cut the base to length on the miter saw; its width sets how many napkins the holder takes, so hold your napkins against it before you cut. Cut the base square at both ends so the assembled holder sits flat.

Drill a 3/8" hole for the dowel tongue jack at the front underside, angled slightly forward the way a real trailer's jack leans, and set the depth so the hole does not break through the opposite face. Cut the dowel so it holds the front of the camper level on a flat table with the base sitting down — that is the jack's actual job here, not decoration, so test the length dry against the assembled body before committing. Glue it in and let it set before handling.

Cut the wheel circles with a hole saw in the drill press, which keeps the cut perpendicular and the circle clean in a way a handheld drill will not. Drill only partway through so each circle reads as a recessed tire rather than a hole punched in the camper; set the depth stop on a test piece in the same stock first. If you are cutting the wheels as separate discs to apply on top instead, back the workpiece with scrap so the exit side does not blow out. These recesses are what make the silhouette read as a specific vintage trailer rather than an abstract shape.

Glue and brad-nail the sides to the base, keeping the gap between them wide enough that a stack of napkins slides in and out without binding but narrow enough that they stand up rather than slump. Add any separate tire pieces the same way, with the nails driven where paint will cover them. Sand every cut edge and soften the corners, then paint in a retro two-tone scheme — the paint is doing as much work as the sawing on a novelty piece like this, so prime first and cut the color break cleanly with tape.

flags: IMPOSSIBLE CUT-LIST ROW: 'Railing, back' is specified as two pieces at 115-3/4" from Pine 1x4, but the only 1x4 listed is 'Pine, 1x4, 8 ft' (96"). A 115-3/4" run cannot be cut from 96" stock, so each back rail must be joined from two pieces. The single targeted rewrite adds that instruction to the railing step; nothing else in this plan needed work.

Otherwise this plan already meets the bar — the steps name the tool, the technique, the fastener, the dimension and the reason (door opening 22" x 38", windows 4-1/2" x 7-1/2" with 1" between, platform support 12-1/4" from the back, slats 2-1/2" apart, slats 3" apart on the railings), and the description deliberately rejects a master cut list in favor of section-by-section cutting. Buildability 4; not rewritten wholesale.
run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box, which is expected — this is three free-standing sections, not a carcass.

Not verified: whole-sheet plywood yield across six 4x8 sheets. The plan explicitly builds section by section rather than from a master layout, so a packed sufficiency claim would contradict the stated method. Not asserted either way.

ops.body: Attach the front, slide, and stair sections together. Build the railings from 1x4 top and bottom rails with 1x2 vertical slats spaced about 3" apart, cutting each run to its own measured length — measure the actual gaps rather than trusting the plan's numbers exactly, since small variations add up. The two back rails run 115-3/4", which is longer than the 8 ft 1x4 stock they come from, so each one has to be joined from two pieces; put the joint over a stud or a post so the splice is backed by something solid and never left spanning open air. Bolt the back railing into wall studs for real load support, and add L-brackets from the inner stair and slide railings down to the under-bed slats, since these two runs see the most side-load from climbing.

baseStepTitles: Extend the front for the mattress
Shape the slide sides and cut closets
Attach the slide platform and slide panel
Attach the stair treads and cut closets
Join the sections and build the railings
Add the tower trim and parapet cutouts

flags: cutList is EMPTY by design — the description explicitly says to size the five pieces to your own half-sheet. The only authored dimensions in the file are the 18in x 26in panel, the 1/2" plywood thickness and the 1-1/4" dowel. The rewrite therefore gives the RELATIONSHIPS between the parts and no invented sizes.

DERIVED and stated in the prose, following the source's own assembly order ('nail one side panel to the bottom', then 'nail both end panels between the two side panels'): the sides lap the bottom's long edges, so the box finishes 1" wider than the bottom; the ends fit between the sides, so each end is as wide as the bottom; and the sides are 1" longer than the bottom. Self-consistent, but it is a derivation, not an authored layout.

SOURCE CONTRADICTION: the handle step says to cut the dowel 'to fit between the two end pieces' and nail through each end into it, but the tool list says the drill-driver 'Drills the handle holes'. Those are two different joints. The rewrite follows the tool note (bored holes) and says so, since a dowel through a bored hole cannot rotate out and is the stronger of the two.

The source never says the end panels must stand taller than the sides, yet the handle is described as sitting 'above the box' — which is only possible if they do. The rewrite states this because the plan is unbuildable without it.
No board-yield claim is made: the panel is 18in x 26in and the piece sizes are the builder's choice, so there is nothing to pack.

ops.body: Lay out a bottom, two sides and two ends on the 18in x 26in half-sheet, and settle the relationships before you settle the sizes, because they determine every cut. The two sides lap the bottom's long edges, so each side is 1" longer than the bottom (1/2" of end panel at each end) and the finished box is 1" wider than the bottom; the two ends then fit between the sides, exactly as wide as the bottom. Cut the two end panels taller than the sides — they have to stand above the box to carry the handle, and nothing else in the build does that job. Draw the whole layout on the panel first and leave a saw kerf between every piece, then cut all five.

Stand a side panel against one long edge of the bottom, flush at both ends and flush along the underside, and nail through the side into the bottom's edge. Do the same on the other long edge. Nailing into the 1/2" plywood edge is the weakest joint in the box, so space the nails every couple of inches, keep them centered on the bottom's thickness, and add glue — a nail that drifts high or low breaks out through a face. Check that the two sides stay parallel as you work; if they splay, the end panels will not drop in square.

Drop the two end panels in between the sides, sitting on the bottom and flush with the ends of the side panels, and nail through the sides into the end panels' edges. Their tall portions should stand up past the sides by the same amount on both ends, since that is what the handle bridges. Check the box for square across its diagonals before the last nails go in — once both ends are fastened the box is fixed, and a racked tool box will rock on a bench forever.

Bore a 1-1/4" hole through each end panel at the same height above the sides, marking both off the same reference edge so the handle sits level. Back each hole with scrap on the exit side so the plywood does not blow out. Cut the dowel long enough to reach the outside face of both ends, tap it through, and nail through each end panel into the dowel with two nails per side — two, not one, because a single nail lets the handle spin in its hole under load, which is exactly how a carry handle fails on something meant to be picked up over and over.

Sand every surface and edge, paying particular attention to the top edges of the sides and the handle itself since those are the parts hands touch. Ease the outside corners so the box does not gouge whatever it gets set down on. Then paint or finish; sealing the plywood's exposed edge grain matters more than the faces, because that is where a shop tool box takes on moisture and starts to swell.

baseStepTitles: Lay out and cut the five pieces
Nail the sides to the bottom
Close the box with the end panels

flags: run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box, but the 54" x 40" back panel proves the case one way and the prose states it with its proof: 52-1/2" bottom plus 3/4" of side stock at each end is exactly 54", and 39-1/4" sides plus a 3/4" top is exactly 40". So the sides run the full height, the bottom sits between them, and the top caps over them. Recorded as a derivation.

MATERIAL IS SUFFICIENT (packBoards plus a hand-checked mixed-length layout): the ten 11-1/4"-wide parts fit exactly the four 1x12s listed — the 10 ft board takes 39-1/4" + 39-1/4" + 33-3/4", and the three 8 ft boards take 57-1/2" + 33-3/4", 52-1/2" + 31-1/2", and 31" + 31" + 31-1/4". The rewrite states this layout because it is the one that fits. The 1x3, 1x6 and 1x2 also each cover their parts exactly.

DOOR WIDTH DEFECT: the three doors are specified 11-1/4" wide (the 1x12's width) and together span 33-3/4", but the case's interior is 52-1/2" less two 3/4" dividers = 51". The doors as listed cannot cover the openings. The plan's own cut-list notes already say 'Cut to fit the actual opening', so the rewrite defers to the real openings and does not assert a door width.

BAY COUNT AMBIGUOUS: the cut list has two 'Dividers' at 33-3/4" and one 'Center divider' at 31-1/2" — three dividers, which makes four bays, against three doors. The source does not resolve this. The rewrite describes fitting the dividers to the door layout you choose rather than asserting a count.

The original steps 2 and 3 read as if the top trim assembly is built twice ('Add the trim' then 'Attach the header assembly'); the rewrite separates the top header (57-1/2" 1x3, matching the top), the bottom header (54" 1x3) and the 5-1/2" x 54" footer, which is what the cut list and the material lengths actually support.
The dividers' vertical positions and the fixed shelf heights are not given anywhere in the source.

step.title: Cut the case parts
step.body: Cut the ten 11-1/4"-wide case and door parts from the 1x12s: a 57-1/2" top, two 39-1/4" sides, a 52-1/2" bottom, two 33-3/4" dividers, a 31-1/2" center divider, two 31" side doors and a 31-1/4" middle door. There is exactly one layout that fits the boards you have — the 10 ft board gives 39-1/4" plus 39-1/4" plus 33-3/4", and the three 8 ft boards give 57-1/2" plus 33-3/4", then 52-1/2" plus 31-1/2", then 31" plus 31" plus 31-1/4" — so follow it rather than cutting the long pieces first and hoping. From the 1x3 cut a 57-1/2" top header and a 54" bottom header, and from the 1x6 a 54" footer. Leave the three doors slightly oversized: the cut list itself says to fit them to the actual openings, and you will not know those until the case is built.

ops.body: Build the case as a plain bookshelf box: the 52-1/2" bottom fits between the two 39-1/4" sides and the 57-1/2" top caps over them. That is what the 54" x 40" back panel proves — 52-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end is 54", and 39-1/4" of side plus a 3/4" top is 40" — and it leaves the top overhanging 1-3/4" past each side, which the crown moulding later sits under. Pocket holes are convenient here but not required; 2" trim screws or 1-1/4" brad nails and glue hold just as well, since the applied moulding does most of the visual work. Stand the two 33-3/4" dividers and the 31-1/2" center divider inside to split the case into your door bays, spacing them to the door layout you have chosen and clamping a spacer between them while you fasten so they do not bow.

Build the top trim as its own assembly on the bench before it goes anywhere near the case — this is the one sequencing trick in the build that genuinely saves time. Join the 57-1/2" 1x3 top header, which matches the top's length, to the 54" 1x3 bottom header with brad nails and glue, keeping them parallel and their faces flush. Getting these aligned as a flat unit on a bench is far easier than fussing with each strip while it is already nailed to a case standing up.

Add the 5-1/2" x 54" 1x6 footer to the assembly, then lift the whole trim unit onto the case and attach it in one operation. Register it to the case's top edge and to one end, and check that it sits level across the full 57-1/2" before you drive more than the first couple of fasteners. Attaching it as a unit is what keeps the top line straight; piece by piece, each strip picks up the last one's error.

Measure each of the three openings in the finished case and cut each door to its own opening — the openings will not be identical and the cut list's stated door sizes are a starting point, not a specification. For a surface-mount door, size each one to overlay its opening evenly on all four sides and leave a consistent gap between neighbors. Mount the Euro-style non-mortise hinges, which need no mortise cut in either the door or the case, then use their adjustment screws to bring the three doors into a straight line across the front; hang all three before you judge any one of them, since a fireplace face reads as a row.

Frame each door opening with the 1x2 trim, mitering or butting the corners consistently and fastening with 1" brad nails and glue. This trim is doing two jobs at once, so treat it as hardware and not just styling: it is what a hand grabs to open the door, so run it where a hand naturally reaches and leave enough of a lip proud of the door face to actually get fingers behind. Nail it into the door itself, not the case, or the doors will not open.

Miter the crown moulding corners and run it around the top, under the top's 1-3/4" overhang, fastening with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Cut and test-fit each outside corner on scrap first — crown is cut on the flat at a compound angle or nested against the saw fence, and a corner that is a degree off shows badly from across a room. Do the same with the 5-1/4" base moulding around the bottom. The substantial base and the crown together are what read as a built-in mantel rather than a bookshelf.

Cut the 1/4" back to 54" x 40", which is the case's actual outside width and height, and glue and nail it to the sides, the bottom, and every divider with 3/4" brad nails. Register a factory edge and corner of the panel to the case and pull the case into line with it as you nail — the back is what squares a 54" case, better than anything you can measure across the diagonal. The back is optional on this build, but without it the case will rack and the door gaps you just adjusted will not stay even.

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf, provable): the 2x4 parts — two 13-1/2" cleats, four 17-1/4" long legs, two 12-1/4" mid pieces and two 7-5/8" short pieces — total 135-3/4" and need TWO 8 ft 2x4s. The plan lists one. packBoards returns boards:2, lowerBound:2, provable:true. The 2x8 and 2x6 are both sufficient.

STEP CONTRADICTION, corrected in the prose: the original step 'Attach the cleats to the tabletop' says to leave 'a consistent 1in overhang at each end', but the top is two 7-1/4" 2x8 boards = 14-1/2" wide and the cleats are 13-1/2" long point to long point, which leaves 1/2" at each end, not 1". The rewrite states 1/2" and shows the arithmetic.
DERIVED and stated in the prose: finished height is 18-3/4" — a 17-1/4" long leg plus the 1-1/2" 2x8 top. The tabletop is 21" x 14-1/2" and the shelf 19" x 11", both from the cut list.

NOT DETERMINED and deliberately not asserted: where the 12-1/4" mid pieces and 7-5/8" short pieces sit within each leg assembly, and which way the 10deg splay runs. The two lengths differ, so they clearly sit at different heights, but nothing in the file says which is high and which is low, or how the assembly registers to the cleats. The rewrite tells the builder to resolve this at the dry-fit the description already calls for.
NOT DETERMINED: the cleats' positions along the top's 21" length, and how far the bottom shelf sits above the floor.
The 'Build the leg assemblies' step tags pocket-hole-jig and combination-square but no drill-driver, which is what actually drives the pocket screws. Corrected in the rewrite.

ops.body: Cut two 21" tabletop boards from the 2x8 and two 19" shelf boards from the 2x6, both square. Everything else is 2x4 and every angled cut is the same 10deg, so set the saw once: two 13-1/2" cleats and two 12-1/4" mid pieces and two 7-5/8" short pieces all measure long point to long point with their ends NOT parallel, while the four 17-1/4" long legs measure long point to short point with their ends parallel — that difference is the whole trick, so mark each blank before you cut it. Count your stock first: those 2x4 parts add up to 135-3/4", so one 8 ft board will not cover them and you need two. Cut one leg, one mid and one short piece, dry-fit them, and confirm the assembly stands the way you want before cutting the other seven — a 2x4 is cheap and a redone leg set after the top is on is not.

Lay the two 21" tabletop boards side by side face down; at 7-1/4" each they make a top 14-1/2" wide. Lay the two 13-1/2" cleats across the underside, running the short way — 13-1/2" of cleat on a 14-1/2" top leaves 1/2" of top proud at each end, so set them in that far on both sides rather than eyeballing it. Orient both cleats so their 10deg ends lean the same way, since that angle is what the leg assemblies register against. Fasten with 2-3/4" self-tapping structural screws and glue, two per board per cleat, driving from the cleat into the tabletop so nothing shows on the finished top.

Build two leg assemblies, each from two 17-1/4" long legs, one 12-1/4" mid piece and one 7-5/8" short piece. The two cross pieces are different lengths because the 10deg splay changes the spacing between the legs at different heights, so dry-clamp one assembly and find where each piece actually lands before you drill anything — the plan does not state which sits high and which sits low, and the dry-fit is what settles it. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the ends of the cross pieces and drive 2-1/2" pocket screws into the legs so every fastener is hidden inside the joint; if you have no jig, 2-3/4" structural screws from the outside face work, just visibly. Build the second assembly against the first as a template so the pair matches.

Stand both leg assemblies against the underside of the tabletop cleats, one at each cleat, with the 10deg faces meeting flush — the cleat angle and the leg angle are the same cut, so a gap here means one of the two is off and it is worth fixing now. Fasten with 2-1/2" pocket screws through the assembly into the cleats. With 17-1/4" legs under a 1-1/2" thick top the table finishes 18-3/4" tall. Turn it upright on a flat floor and check that it does not rock before adding the shelf.

Set the two 19" 2x6 shelf boards between the leg assemblies, side by side to make a shelf 11" wide, resting on the lower cross pieces. Clamp them in place and check the table for square and for rock before fastening — the shelf is what stops the splayed legs from spreading, so whatever position it locks in is the position the table keeps. Fasten from underneath with 2-3/4" structural screws and glue so no screw head shows on the shelf surface.

Sand every surface and ease all the edges and corners, particularly the tabletop's ends and the outside faces of the legs, since this piece is built to double as a stool and gets handled. Vacuum and wipe the dust off before finishing or the stain will carry it. Apply the stain to match the rest of the Homestead Collection, finishing the underside and the shelf's bottom face as well as the visible surfaces so the boards take on moisture evenly.

baseStepTitles: Attach the cleats to the tabletop
Attach the legs to the top
